description: Structured source registry and occurrence database for the diachronic semantic history of χριστός / Christos across Classical, Hellenistic, Roman, and Early Church Greek.

lexica.full_title: Liddell, Scott, Jones — A Greek-English Lexicon

entry_summary.I: Of things: 'to be rubbed on, used as ointment or salve' — opp. πιστός (Aesch. Pr. 480; Eur. Hipp. 516)
entry_summary.IIa: Of persons: anointed priests — LXX Lev 4:5, 4:16, 6:22
entry_summary.IIb: Kings of Israel: χριστὸς κυρίου — LXX 1 Sam 24:7; Ps 17:51
entry_summary.III: NT: ὁ χ. = the Messiah (Heb. מָשִׁיחַ) — as title Matt 2:4; as proper name Matt 1:1

lexica.notes: LSJ gives minimal treatment to NT and patristic usage; Thayer's NT lexicon supplements for those stages.

lexica.full_title: Thayer, J.H. — A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament

entry_summary.1: ὁ Χριστός as Jewish messianic title (the expected deliverer)
entry_summary.2: Applied to Jesus as fulfillment of OT messianic role
entry_summary.3: Title becoming proper name: Ἰησοῦς Χριστός
entry_summary.4: ψευδόχριστοι (false Christs) — confirms titular technical status

lexica.full_title: Brown, Driver, Briggs — A Hebrew and English Lexicon of the Old Testament
lexica.entry_summary: māšaḥ = to anoint/smear (verb); māšîaḥ = anointed one (qal passive ptcp. substantivized). Primary referents: priests (Lev), kings (Sam/Ps), Cyrus (Isa 45:1), future leader (Dan 9:25–26).
lexica.notes: Hebrew root מָשַׁח is structurally parallel to Greek χρίω; both yield verbal adjectives meaning 'anointed one.'

stages.name: Classical Greek — Pharmaceutical Adjective
stages.date_range: c. 460–1st century BCE (and continuing in medical prose)
stages.paradigmatic_opposition: χριστά ≠ πιστά / ποτά (topical vs. drinkable medicines)
stages.morphological_status: Verbal adjective (from χρίω); modifies THINGS not persons

key_texts.author: Aeschylus
key_texts.authorship_note: DISPUTED: M.L. West (JHS 99, 1979) and M. Griffith (1983) argue post-Aeschylean authorship; attribution to Aeschylus is traditional but contested.
key_texts.translation: medicines … smearable and drinkable

key_texts.author: Euripides
key_texts.authorship_note: Securely Euripidean; 428 BCE performance date recorded by ancient vita.
key_texts.greek_text: πότερα δὲ χριστὸν ἢ ποτὸν τὸ φάρμακον;
key_texts.translation: Is the drug a smearable [salve] or a drinkable [potion]?

key_texts.author: Hippocratic Corpus
key_texts.morphological_note: CAUTION: De ulceribus 18 (Perseus:1999.01.0248) uses χρηστέον (gerundive from χράομαι 'to use', not from χρίω 'to anoint'). These are distinct roots. Genuine χριστά forms (from χρίω) do appear in the Hippocratic corpus but in passages not retrieved from open-access text. Full TLG query needed.
key_texts.source: LSJ s.v. χρίω (related entries); medical text tradition

stages.name: LXX Hellenistic — Priestly Anointing Title
stages.semantic_function: Institutional title: 'the anointed priest' — renders H. הַכֹּהֵן הַמָּשִׁיחַ
stages.translation_mechanism: Structural calque: Greek χριστός chosen as morphological equivalent of Hebrew מָשִׁיחַ (both = verbal adj. of 'anoint')
stages.morphological_status: Attributive adjective in frozen title construction: ὁ ἱερεὺς ὁ χριστός

key_texts.greek_text: ὁ ἱερεὺς ὁ χριστὸς ὁ τετελειωμένος τὰς χεῖρας
key_texts.translation: the anointed priest who has been ordained
the anointed priest

key_texts.greek_text: ὁ ἱερεὺς ὁ χριστὸς ἀντ᾿ αὐτοῦ
key_texts.translation: the anointed priest who succeeds him
the anointing oil
key_texts.note: Bridges Stage 1 and Stage 2a: χριστός still modifies an object (oil) but in a religious ritual context

key_texts.translation: the anointed high priest
key_texts.note: Perfect passive participle κεχρισμένος (NOT the adjective χριστός) — both render the same Hebrew הַמָּשִׁיחַ; LXX translators alternate between the two forms without semantic distinction.

stages.name: LXX Hellenistic — Royal Davidic Title
stages.date_range: Historical books and Psalms, translation c. 200–150 BCE
stages.semantic_function: Royal title: 'the LORD's anointed' — renders H. מְשִׁיחַ יְהוָה / מְשִׁיחִי
stages.morphological_status: Near-formulaic construction: χριστὸς κυρίου (nom.) / χριστοῦ αὐτοῦ (gen.) / τῷ χριστῷ (dat.)

key_texts.translation: will exalt the horn of his anointed
key_texts.note: Hannah's prayer — reference is PROSPECTIVE (no king yet); one of the earliest LXX passages with any forward-looking force for χριστός.

key_texts.grammatical_case: Dat. sg. (1st use); Nom. sg. predicate (2nd use)
key_texts.greek_text: τῷ χριστῷ κυρίου … χριστὸς κυρίου ἐστὶν οὗτος
key_texts.translation: to the LORD's anointed … because this is the LORD's anointed
key_texts.note: PARADIGMATIC royal use: Saul as current χριστὸς κυρίου; the formula χριστὸς κυρίου ἐστίν is the clearest predicative title construction.

key_texts.greek_text: κατὰ τοῦ κυρίου καὶ κατὰ τοῦ χριστοῦ αὐτοῦ
key_texts.translation: against the LORD and against his anointed
key_texts.nt_application: Acts 4:25–26 applies this verse to Jesus and Herod/Pilate
key_texts.note: Most-cited LXX messianic verse in NT. Its 'royal' reference becomes proto-messianic through re-reading.

key_texts.grammatical_case: Dat. sg. masc. (indirect object, in apposition to Κύρῳ)
key_texts.translation: to my anointed — Cyrus
key_texts.note: UNIQUE: only LXX application of χριστός to a non-Israelite. Decouples the title from Davidic lineage. Proves χριστός = functionally 'divinely authorized agent' not 'ethnic insider.'

key_texts.grammatical_case: Masc. acc. PLURAL — only plural instance in LSJ LXX citation
key_texts.translation: your anointed ones
key_texts.note: Collective/plural royal use; NT NEVER uses plural χριστοί in this way — the NT singularizes the title exclusively onto Jesus.

key_texts.greek_text: χριστὸς κυρίου συνελήμφθη ἐν ταῖς διαφθοραῖς αὐτῶν
key_texts.translation: the LORD's anointed was caught in their traps

stages.name: LXX Hellenistic — Proto-Messianic (Future Reference)
stages.date_range: Daniel translation c. 100 BCE; Theodotion revision c. 2nd century CE
stages.semantic_function: Future-pointing anointed figure in a numerical schema

key_texts.translation: until an anointed leader, seven weeks
key_texts.note: Anarthrous — signals proto-name status. Future referent within '70 weeks' schema. Foundational for Second Temple messianic chronology.

textual_variants.LXX: ἐξολοθρευθήσεται χρῖσμα (= 'an anointing will be cut off')
textual_variants.Theodotion: ἐξολοθρευθήσεται χριστός (= 'an anointed one will be cut off')

key_texts.note: DISPUTED TEXT: LXX and Theodotion diverge on whether the referent is the abstract noun χρῖσμα or the title χριστός. Theodotion's version becomes standard in Christian citation. The variant itself shows LXX translators navigating the title/abstract-noun boundary.

stages.name: Second Temple Jewish — Explicitly Eschatological Messianic Title
stages.semantic_function: Title for a FUTURE Davidic deliverer — not a current reigning king
stages.note: NOT IN PROJECT FILES — critical missing evidence

key_texts.edition: Ed. Rahlfs; R.B. Wright in OTP vol.2 (Charlesworth, 1985)
key_texts.translation: their king, the Lord's anointed
key_texts.note: CRUCIAL: the king is FUTURE (no Davidic king reigns; Hasmoneans considered illegitimate). χριστὸς κυρίου without article — title is shedding the article = moving toward name-status. This is genuine eschatological messianism pre-dating the NT.
key_texts.significance: Strongest pre-NT evidence for χριστός as a genuinely future eschatological title

key_texts.translation: under the rod of discipline of the Lord's anointed

stages.name: New Testament — Paul (earliest layer)
stages.semantic_function: Proper name / title in transition; ἐν Χριστῷ formula; creedal uses

key_texts.greek_text: ἐν θεῷ πατρὶ καὶ κυρίῳ Ἰησοῦ Χριστῷ
key_texts.translation: in God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ
key_texts.note: Earliest datable Christian document. Compound name Ἰησοῦς Χριστός fully established.

key_texts.grammatical_case: Nom. sg. — ANARTHROUS subject of main verb
key_texts.greek_text: Χριστὸς ἀπέθανεν ὑπὲρ τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν ἡμῶν κατὰ τὰς γραφάς
key_texts.translation: Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures
key_texts.note: Received pre-Pauline tradition (παρέδωκα … ὃ παρέλαβον). Anarthrous Χριστός as subject = proper name. INFERENCE that underlying tradition dates c.35–40 CE (Dunn, Theology of Paul, pp.235–238).

key_texts.translation: Christ crucified — a stumbling block to Jews
key_texts.note: Paul explicitly acknowledges that 'Christ crucified' is a RESEMANTICIZATION of the messianic title: crucifixion ≠ the expected messianic victory (σκάνδαλον = scandal).

key_texts.translation: you are all one in Christ Jesus
key_texts.note: ἐν Χριστῷ formula (~164 occurrences in Pauline corpus). Locative dative with a person's name = the name is fully onomastic.

stages.name: New Testament — Synoptic Gospels
stages.semantic_function: Both title (ὁ Χριστός) and proper name (Ἰησοῦς Χριστός)

key_texts.greek_text: Βίβλος γενέσεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ, υἱοῦ Δαυίδ
key_texts.translation: The book of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, son of David
key_texts.note: Incipit: Χριστός without article = proper name (cognomen).

key_texts.greek_text: Σὺ εἶ ὁ Χριστὸς ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ τοῦ ζῶντος
key_texts.translation: You are the Christ, the Son of the living God
key_texts.note: Peter's confession: ὁ Χριστός with article = live messianic title in narrative context.

key_texts.translation: false Christs and false prophets
key_texts.note: COMPOUND ψευδόχριστοι = 'false Christs.' Proves χριστός sufficiently technical that a category of false claimants is conceivable — title formation is complete.

stages.name: New Testament — Johannine Literature
stages.semantic_function: Bilingual gloss; both title and name; stated Gospel purpose

key_texts.greek_text: Εὑρήκαμεν τὸν Μεσσίαν, ὅ ἐστιν μεθερμηνευόμενον ὁ Χριστός
key_texts.translation: We have found the Messiah, which is translated 'the Christ'
key_texts.note: KEY PASSAGE: ONLY NT passage with explicit bilingual translation gloss. Establishes: Μεσσίας (Aramaic loanword) = ὁ Χριστός (Greek calque). The formula μεθερμηνευόμενον ('which is translated') is the metalinguistic marker.

key_texts.translation: The Messiah is coming, the one called Christ
key_texts.note: Second bilingual gloss in John; ὁ λεγόμενος Χριστός = same distancing/explanatory formula as Josephus Ant. 20.200. Here explanatory for Greek readers, not distancing.

key_texts.greek_text: ἵνα πιστεύ[σ]ητε ὅτι Ἰησοῦς ἐστιν ὁ Χριστὸς ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ
key_texts.translation: that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God
key_texts.note: Gospel's stated purpose: the identification 'Jesus = the Christ' is the organizing theological claim. ὁ Χριστός with double article = maximum definiteness.

key_texts.author: Josephus
key_texts.greek_text: τὸν ἀδελφὸν Ἰησοῦ τοῦ λεγομένου Χριστοῦ, Ἰάκωβος ὄνομα αὐτῷ
key_texts.translation: the brother of Jesus who was called Christ, James by name
key_texts.authenticity: AUTHENTIC (widely accepted by scholars; no Christian interpolation suspected)
key_texts.note: τοῦ λεγομένου Χριστοῦ: Josephus assumes his readers KNOW what 'Christ' designates and uses λεγόμενος ('so-called') to distance himself from endorsement — not to explain an obscure term. Confirms name-status by 93 CE.
key_texts.source: Niese ed.; Oxford Academic on Ant.20.200

key_texts.author: Josephus
key_texts.translation: he was the Christ
key_texts.authenticity: DISPUTED: current form reflects probable Christian embellishment; no surviving 'neutral' Greek manuscript. Modern reconstructions are scholarly hypotheses.
key_texts.source: Niese ed.; Meier, Marginal Jew vol.1, pp.56–88

stages.name: Early Church — Fixed Proper Name and Theological Expansion
stages.semantic_function: Fixed proper name; generates new vocabulary (Χριστιανισμός); requires etymological explanation for non-Christian audiences
stages.note: NOT IN PROJECT FILES — new evidence added in diachronic analysis

key_texts.author: 1 Clement
key_texts.greek_text: …κλητοῖς ἡγιασμένοις ἐν θελήματι θεοῦ διὰ τοῦ κυρίου ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ
key_texts.translation: to the called, sanctified by the will of God through our Lord Jesus Christ
key_texts.note: Χριστός is purely formulaic in 1 Clement salutation — no messianic explanation needed; it IS the proper name.

key_texts.author: Ignatius of Antioch

key_forms.name_formula: κυρίου Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (salutation) — proper name compound
key_forms.partitive_gen: ὅσοι γὰρ θεοῦ εἰσιν καὶ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (3:2)
key_forms.neologism: Χριστιανισμός (6:1) — abstract noun from the proper name
key_forms.locative: ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ τῇ κοινῇ ἐλπίδι ἡμῶν (11:2)

key_texts.note: Χριστιανισμός (Phld.6:1; Magn.10:1–3) is a NEOLOGISM: you cannot form '-ισμός' abstracts from titles, only from proper names. This SEALS Stage 5.

key_texts.author: Justin Martyr
key_texts.note: Justin defends the NAME 'Χριστιανοί'. Plays on confusion: Χριστιανοί (from Χριστός = 'anointed') vs. χρηστοί / Χρηστιανοί (from χρηστός = 'good'). The fact that Justin must ARGUE that the name is not blameworthy, and must distinguish it from a similarly-sounding Greek adjective, proves Χριστός is an OPAQUE PROPER NAME to non-Christian Greek speakers by c.155 CE.

key_texts.author: Justin Martyr
key_texts.note: Justin explains that Jesus is called χριστός because he was 'anointed' (κεχρισμένος) as king, priest, and prophet. This ETYMOLOGICAL RECOVERY — explaining to an audience WHY a word means what it means — is the definitive sign that χριστός has become an opaque proper name.

question_1_borrowing_model.verdict: INACCURATE to describe as 'borrowing into Hebrew and back into Greek'
question_1_borrowing_model.correct_model: SEMANTIC CALQUE: Greek χριστός chosen as structural translation equivalent for Hebrew מָשִׁיחַ; Hebrew never borrowed the Greek word; direction of semantic transfer is Hebrew → Greek through LXX translation; Μεσσίας (John 1:41) is a separate LOANWORD channel (Aramaic → Greek)

semantic_stages_summary: Stage 2a: Priestly title via LXX calque — LEXICAL TRANSLATION
Stage 2b: Royal formulaic title — TITLE FORMATION
Stage 3: Explicit future messianic (PsSol) — ESCHATOLOGICAL TITLE
Stage 4: NT — name/title in transition, resemanticized — RESEMANTICIZATION
Stage 5: Early church — fixed proper name — ONOMASTIC
strongest_evidence_for_name_transition: Anarthrous Χριστός as sentence subject: 1 Cor 15:3
Need to explain etymology: Justin 1 Apol. 4
open_questions: Hippocratic corpus genuine χριστ- forms require full TLG credential query
χριστοί nom. pl. masc. not separately confirmed in open-source text
LXX total occurrence count: ~38–40 (not 40–50 as in project files)
Justin Dial. 86 exact Greek requires Marcovich critical edition
